flashcards.Back: A QoS term referring to the specific treatment (like priority or dropping) a single router gives to a packet as it passes through.
The process of identifying and grouping network traffic into different categories based on header fields like IP addresses or port numbers.
The action of changing a field in a packet or frame header (e.g., DSCP or CoS) to label its priority level for other devices.
The management of memory buffers where packets are stored when an outgoing interface is busy, ensuring high-priority traffic is sent first.
QoS tools that proactively drop packets when buffers fill up to prevent total congestion and maintain network stability.
A traffic management tool that enforces a strict bit rate by immediately dropping any packets that exceed the configured limit.
A technique that smooths traffic bursts by buffering excess packets in a queue and sending them at a constant, lower rate.
The location in a network where a device (like an access switch) begins to accept and act upon the QoS markings present in incoming packets.
Differentiated Services Code Point. A 6-bit field in the IP header used for advanced traffic classification, providing up to 64 priority levels.
A 3-bit marking field in the 802.1Q Ethernet header, used to prioritize traffic at Layer 2 across trunk links.
A high-priority DSCP value (46) typically used for VoIP traffic to ensure low delay, low jitter, and guaranteed bandwidth.
A set of DSCP values that provide guaranteed delivery but allow for different drop probabilities if the network becomes congested.
A Cisco queuing method that provides a strict-priority queue for real-time traffic (voice/video) alongside standard bandwidth-guaranteed queues.
A condition where a router drops all incoming packets for a specific queue because the buffer is completely full.
The variation in the delay of received packets. QoS tools like LLQ are used to minimize jitter for time-sensitive applications.
The minimum data rate required by an application. QoS ensures that critical applications always have their required bandwidth available.
The average traffic rate that a service provider or shaper guarantees to transport under normal conditions.
Network-Based Application Recognition. A deep packet inspection technology used for advanced traffic classification up to the application layer.
First-In, First-Out. The simplest queuing method where packets are sent in the exact order they arrive, providing no prioritization.
A method where higher-priority queues are always emptied before lower-priority ones, which can lead to 'bandwidth starvation' for low-priority traffic.
CBWFQ. A method that guarantees a minimum amount of bandwidth to different traffic classes while sharing excess capacity fairly.
A legacy 3-bit field in the IP header used for marking, which has been superseded by the more granular 6-bit DSCP field.
Another name for the CoS bits in an 802.1Q or 802.1ad header used for Layer 2 prioritization.
A set of DSCP values designed to be backward compatible with the older 3-bit IP Precedence values.
WRED. A congestion avoidance tool that randomly drops low-priority packets before a buffer becomes full to prevent TCP global synchronization.
A network inefficiency where multiple TCP flows all reduce their window sizes at once due to tail drop, leading to oscillating link utilization.
The default treatment for network traffic where no QoS guarantees are applied. it is typically marked with a DSCP value of 0.
The failure of one or more transmitted packets to reach their destination. QoS aims to minimize this for critical real-time traffic.
The logic used by a router to decide which packet from which queue to send out of an interface next.
A QoS process that checks if enough network resources are available before allowing a new high-priority session (like a VoIP call) to start.
